name: National Education Union
description: The largest education union in the UK and indeed Europe. Came about as a merger between the National Union of Teachers and Association of Teachers and Lecturers in 2017. Known for collective action, national campaigning and a wide in-school network of volunteer reps. Covers teachers, leaders and support staff across England, Wales and Northern Ireland. Affiliated to the TUC.
strength_1: Largest education union — strongest collective voice
strength_2: Active on pay, workload and education policy
strength_3: In-school reps in many secondary and primary schools
strength_4: Covers teachers and support staff in the same union
strength_5: Active social media and online presence
strength_6: Effective at mobilising members on campaigns relative to other unions
consideration_1: Politically active — campaigns beyond education issues
consideration_2: Local branch subs added on top of the national rate
consideration_3: Considered to be the most 'militant' of the teaching unions i.e. more likely to call for industrial action
consideration_4: Representation quality may vary locally with speed of support depending on the strength of the local branch.
consideration_5: Considered by GMB, Unison and Unite to have gone against a TUC agreement to not recruit support staff

name: NASUWT — The Teachers' Union
description: A specialist teachers' union and TUC affiliate covering all four UK nations. Focuses on workplace rights, pay and conditions. Often seen as the slightly calmer sister union to the NEU but still willing to take industrial action.
strength_1: One transparent national fee — no local levies
strength_3: Active across England, Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland
strength_4: Strong emphasis on pay, workload and conditions
strength_6: Free CPD on offer for members
consideration_1: Teachers and leaders only — not open to support staff
consideration_2: Smaller than NEU in many English schools but has a broader reach across all 4 home nations.
consideration_3: Some controversy over General Secretary not coming from an education background
consideration_4: Level of represenation varies at local level and quality of support will depend heavily on local reps and officers
consideration_5: Struggled to meet minimum ballot thresholds for previous national industrial action votes

name: Edapt
description: Relatively modern offering compared to traditional unions as only founded in 2012. Styles itself as the apolitical alternative to a teaching union. For those who want the type of support and advice that comes from education unions but are not intersted in the wider campaigning and politics. Only operates in England and Wales. Focuses on supporting members as individuals rather than as collective groups. Supports anybody working in a school environment including support staff.
strength_1: Only uses professional caseworkers — not volunteer reps which some consider to be more specialist.
strength_2: Apolitical — no campaigning or political affiliation so may suit staff who are just looking for protection and nothing else
strength_3: 24/7 mental health support line (only one other union offers this)
strength_4: Whilst they can't vote in other unions' ballots, members can still join strikes as non-union participants if they want to have their voice heard
strength_5: Cheapest membership cost in most instances as no fees spent on campaigning etc
strength_6: Public reviews of its support are excellent
consideration_1: Cannot organise or ballot for industrial action
consideration_4: Not as large as established trade unions in terms of membership numbers
consideration_5: Some trade unionists argue that their existence undermines the union movement

name: Association of School and College Leaders
description: A leadership-focused union for senior leaders in schools and colleges. Offers specialist CPD, legal support and policy influence tailored to senior roles. Covers England, Wales and Northern Ireland. History dating back to 1874.
strength_1: Highly specialist focus on senior leaders and those with strategic responsibility
strength_2: Seen as a considered voice and listened to by policy makers at government level
strength_3: Tailored legal advice for leadership roles
strength_4: Professional caseworkers and hotline to support staff rather than in-school volunteers
strength_5: Has formal corporate membership links with HMC and GSA, giving it credibility and reach in the independent senior school sector.
consideration_1: Leaders only — not open to classroom teachers or support staff
consideration_3: Not a TUC affiliate and politically neutral
consideration_4: Smaller than main leadership alternative union NAHT
consideration_5: Seen as slightly less political than the NAHT

name: National Association of Head Teachers
description: A union specifically targetting school leaders and wider leadership roles. Active on school funding, accountability and headteacher-specific issues. Long history going back to 1897.
strength_3: Professional caseworkers and hotline to support staff rather than in-school volunteers
strength_5: The largest of the leadership specific teaching unions
consideration_3: Separate NAHT NI branch for Northern Ireland

name: Community Union
description: A general trade union that has political links to the Labour Party. Represents workers in a variety of industries including steelworkers, garment workers and social work employees. 'Voice' a former specialist education union known for its independence and non-striking policy transferred to become part of Community in 2020. The union
strength_1: Broad education and early years coverage
strength_3: Potentially attractive for early years and non-traditional education roles
strength_4: Covers support staff across the UK
strength_5: Voice heritage - known as a relatively moderate, non-strike education union
consideration_1: Not education-specific although does have an education and early years section
consideration_2: Less visible in most schools compared to larger unions
consideration_4: Voice's former non-strike identity may be less clear now

name: GMB Union
description: One of the UK's largest general unions and a TUC affiliate. Covers school support staff. GMB describes itself as “the union for school support staff” and says it has more than 100,000 members working in schools. Strong on public sector pay and workplace rights.
strength_1: Large union with collective bargaining power
strength_3: Only focuses on support staff in schools, not teachers so seen as more specialist for them
strength_4: Covers support staff across all four nations
strength_5: Part of the National Joint Council that helps to negotiate pay for local authority support staff
consideration_3: Reps may not be education specialists

name: UNISON
description: The UK's largest public sector union and a TUC affiliate. Strong presence in schools for support staff — TAs, admin, caretakers — with a dedicated education sector team. Has over 250,000 school support staff members. Also affiliated to the Labour Party.
strength_1: Largest public sector union — significant bargaining power
strength_4: Fees scale with earnings — low-paid staff pay little
strength_5: Recognised beyond schools and part of the
strength_6: Part of the National Joint Council that helps to negotiate pay for local authority support staff
consideration_2: Not education-specific with a broader focus on other industries
consideration_3: Earnings-based fees can be higher for better-paid roles
consideration_4: Is affiliated to the Labour Party.

name: Unite the Union
description: One of the UK's largest general unions, with over 1 million members across many sectors including some school support staff (around 250k). Covers TAs, admin, facilities and similar roles. Politically active and a Labour Party affiliate.
strength_1: Large general union with significant bargaining power
strength_2: Active on pay and conditions across the public sector
strength_4: Covers support staff across all four nations
strength_5: Part of the National Joint Council that helps to negotiate pay for local authority support staff
consideration_2: Most school members are reached through general regional structures rather than dedicated school presence
consideration_4: Politically active and affiliated to the Labour Party

name: Educational Institute of Scotland
description: Scotland's largest teaching union and a founding STUC affiliate. Covers teachers and lecturers across Scottish schools, colleges and universities. Very active on pay and conditions in Scotland.
strength_2: Strong collective bargaining in Scotland's SNCT
strength_3: Active on pay, workload and policy
strength_5: Scotland specific with long professional history
strength_6: Specific independent schools section for teachers in Scotland
consideration_2: Teachers / lecturers / leaders — not support staff
consideration_4: Local support still depends on workplace and branch capacity

name: Association of Headteachers and Deputes in Scotland
description: The professional association and recognised trade union for primary, special and nursery school leaders in Scotland. Specialist casework, CPD and representation for Scottish school leaders.
strength_1: Specialist support for Scottish primary and special-school leaders
consideration_2: Primary, special and nursery leaders only — not secondary

name: School Leaders Scotland
description: The professional association and recognised trade union for secondary school leaders in Scotland — present in over 90% of Scottish secondary schools and representing around 75% of headteachers. Fully affiliated to ASCL, which gives members full trade union rights and access to ASCL's wider legal infrastructure. Sits alongside AHDS (which serves primary, special and nursery leaders).
strength_1: Specialist support for Scottish secondary school leaders
strength_2: Dedicated field officer support and leadership-specific casework
strength_3: Policy access in Scottish secondary education — parliamentary bulletins, position statements
strength_4: ASCL affiliation provides legal advice and broader trade union backing
consideration_3: Less industrial-action presence than larger teacher unions

name: Scottish Secondary Teachers' Association
description: Scotland's only specialist union for secondary teachers, founded in 1944. Twin aims are to advance Scottish education and safeguard secondary teachers' interests, especially on pay and conditions. Active in SNCT pay negotiations alongside EIS and NASUWT..
strength_2: Strong representation in Scottish secondary schools
strength_4: 16 months free for new entrants — generous trainee/NQT offer
consideration_2: Secondary teachers (and trainees) only — not for leaders or primary teachers
consideration_3: Smaller than EIS — less broad sector representation
consideration_4: Has a political fund framework, though no public evidence of party donations
cost_5_value: Free for 16 months from August of qualifying year

name: Irish National Teachers' Organisation
description: The largest teachers' union on the island of Ireland. In Northern Ireland it represents teachers and principals across both primary and post-primary (secondary) schools, with strong casework and professional development.
strength_1: Cross-border presence — strong in NI primary, post-primary and special schools
strength_2: Salary-based subscription deducted directly from payroll
strength_4: Free for trainees and newly qualified teachers in the first year
consideration_1: Northern Ireland only (separate INTO branches operate in Republic of Ireland)
consideration_2: Teachers and leaders only — not for non-teaching support staff
consideration_3: Salary-based fee means cost rises with pay scale
cost_4_value: Pro-rata on days worked (0.6% of pay)

name: Ulster Teachers' Union
description: A Northern Ireland teaching union with a focus on individual member support and a notably apolitical stance. Covers teachers and school leaders across NI. Describes itself as the only union based solely in NI for the teachers of NI.
strength_2: Partnership with INTO designed to increase membership benefits
consideration_2: Smaller than INTO or NASUWT NI with around 7000 members

name: Undeb Cenedlaethol Athrawon Cymru
description: The Welsh-medium and Wales-focused teaching union. Bilingual service, strong on Welsh education policy, and casework delivered through the medium of Welsh or English.
strength_2: Specialist voice in Welsh education policy
consideration_2: Smaller than NEU Cymru or NASUWT Cymru with around 3500 members
